questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor with a plate area of $25.0 \text{ cm}^2$ and a $2.00 \text{ mm}$ separation between its plates is connected to a $12.0 \text{ V}$ battery. Determine the charge on the capacitor.
questions.solution:
The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is given by $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$

The charge on the capacitor is $Q = CV = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V}{d}$

Substituting the given values:

$A = 25.0 \text{ cm}^2 = 25.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

$d = 2.00 \text{ mm} = 2.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$V = 12.0 \text{ V}$

$\varepsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

$Q = \dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 25.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 12.0}{2.00 \times 10^{-3}}$

$Q = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 25.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.0 \times 10^3$

$Q = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 150 \times 10^{-1}$

$Q = 132.75 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}$

$Q \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the charges on the three capacitors connected to a battery as depicted in the figure. Assume $C_1 = 2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $C_2 = 4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $C_3 = 6.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, and $V = 12$ volts.
questions.solution:
From the given circuit diagram, it can be observed that all three capacitors $C_1$, $C_2$, and $C_3$ are connected in parallel across the battery $V$.

In a parallel circuit, the potential difference across each component is the same. Therefore, the voltage across each capacitor is equal to the battery voltage, $V = 12 \text{ V}$.

The charge on a capacitor is given by the formula $Q = CV$.

For capacitor $C_1$:
$Q_1 = C_1 V = 2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 12 \text{ V} = 24 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

For capacitor $C_2$:
$Q_2 = C_2 V = 4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 12 \text{ V} = 48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

For capacitor $C_3$:
$Q_3 = C_3 V = 6.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 12 \text{ V} = 72 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If one intends to build a $1.0\text{ farad}$ capacitor using circular discs separated by a distance of $1.0\text{ mm}$, what must be the radius of the discs?
questions.solution:
The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given by $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$.

For circular discs of radius $r$, the area is $A = \pi r^2$.

Substituting the area into the capacitance formula gives $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi r^2}{d}$.

Rearranging for $r^2$, we get $r^2 = \dfrac{C d}{\pi \varepsilon_0}$.

We know that $\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\pi \varepsilon_0} = 36 \times 10^9$.

Substituting the given values $C = 1.0\text{ F}$ and $d = 1.0\text{ mm} = 10^{-3}\text{ m}$:

$r^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 36 \times 10^9 = 36 \times 10^6\text{ m}^2$

$r = \sqrt{36 \times 10^6} = 6000\text{ m} = 6\text{ km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor possessing a plate area of $25.0 \text{ cm}^2$ and a plate separation of $2.00 \text{ mm}$ is connected to a $12.0 \text{ V}$ battery. If the separation between the plates is subsequently reduced to $1.00 \text{ mm}$, determine the extra charge supplied by the battery to the positive plate.
questions.solution:
Initial capacitance $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_1}$

Final capacitance $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_2}$

Since the battery remains connected, the potential difference $V$ across the capacitor is constant.

Initial charge $Q_1 = C_1 V = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V}{d_1}$

Final charge $Q_2 = C_2 V = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V}{d_2}$

Extra charge supplied $\Delta Q = Q_2 - Q_1 = \varepsilon_0 A V \left( \dfrac{1}{d_2} - \dfrac{1}{d_1} \right)$

Substituting the given values:
$A = 25.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$d_1 = 2.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$d_2 = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$V = 12.0 \text{ V}$
$\varepsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

$\Delta Q = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 25.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 12.0 \times \left( \dfrac{1}{1.00 \times 10^{-3}} - \dfrac{1}{2.00 \times 10^{-3}} \right)$

$\Delta Q = 265.5 \times 10^{-15} \times (1000 - 500)$

$\Delta Q = 265.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 500 = 1.3275 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C}$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $\Delta Q \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A potential difference of $10 \text{ V}$ develops between two conductors when $1.0 \times 10^{12}$ electrons are transferred from one conductor to the other. Determine the capacitance of the two-conductor system.
questions.solution:
The charge transferred from one conductor to the other is given by $q = ne$.

Substituting the given values:

$q = (1.0 \times 10^{12}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \text{ C} = 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$

The capacitance of the system is given by $C = \dfrac{q}{V}$.

Substituting $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$ and $V = 10 \text{ V}$:

$C = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{10} \text{ F} = 1.6 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Calculate the charge appearing on each of the three capacitors shown in the diagram.
options: $24 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on each of the $4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors
$48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $24 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on each of the $4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors
$96 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on each of the $4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors
$48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on each of the $4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors
questions.solution:
From the given circuit diagram, capacitors B and C are in parallel.

Equivalent capacitance of B and C is:
$C_{BC} = 4 \text{ } \mu\text{F} + 4 \text{ } \mu\text{F} = 8 \text{ } \mu\text{F}$

Capacitor A is in series with this parallel combination. The equivalent capacitance of the entire circuit is:
$C_{eq} = \dfrac{8 \times 8}{8 + 8} = 4 \text{ } \mu\text{F}$

Total charge drawn from the battery is:
$Q = C_{eq}V = 4 \text{ } \mu\text{F} \times 12 \text{ V} = 48 \text{ } \mu\text{C}$

Since capacitor A is in the main branch, the charge on the $8 \text{ } \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is $48 \text{ } \mu\text{C}$.

This charge of $48 \text{ } \mu\text{C}$ distributes equally between the two identical $4 \text{ } \mu\text{F}$ capacitors in parallel.

Charge on each $4 \text{ } \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:
$q = \dfrac{48 \text{ } \mu\text{C}}{2} = 24 \text{ } \mu\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $C_1 = 4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $C_2 = 6.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ in the figure. Determine the equivalent capacitance of the combinations (a) and (b) between the indicated points.
questions.solution:
In circuit (a), the arrangement consists of two parallel groups connected in series.

The first parallel group has $C_1$ and $C_2$, giving an equivalent capacitance $C_{p1} = 4.0 + 6.0 = 10.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

The second parallel group also has $C_1$ and $C_2$, giving $C_{p2} = 4.0 + 6.0 = 10.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

The total equivalent capacitance for (a) is the series combination of $C_{p1}$ and $C_{p2}$:

$C_a = \dfrac{10.0 \times 10.0}{10.0 + 10.0} = 5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

In circuit (b), the arrangement consists of two branches connected in parallel across the main terminals. Each branch is identical to circuit (a).

The equivalent capacitance of the top branch is $5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and the bottom branch is $5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

The total equivalent capacitance for (b) is the parallel combination of these two branches:

$C_b = 5.0 + 5.0 = 10.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

Answer: (a) $5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, (b) $10.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the charge supplied by the battery in the arrangement presented in the figure.
questions.solution:
Let the nodes of the circuit be identified to determine the combination of capacitors.

The positive terminal of the $10\text{ V}$ battery is connected to the left plate of the $5.0\text{ \mu F}$ capacitor and the right plate of the $6.0\text{ \mu F}$ capacitor.

The negative terminal of the battery is connected to the right plate of the $5.0\text{ \mu F}$ capacitor and the left plate of the $6.0\text{ \mu F}$ capacitor.

Since both capacitors are connected directly across the same potential difference of $10\text{ V}$, they are in a parallel combination.

The equivalent capacitance of the circuit is $C_{\text{eq}} = C_1 + C_2 = 5.0\text{ \mu F} + 6.0\text{ \mu F} = 11.0\text{ \mu F}$.

The total charge supplied by the battery is given by $Q = C_{\text{eq}} V$.

Substituting the values, $Q = 11.0\text{ \mu F} \times 10\text{ V} = 110\text{ \mu C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two cylindrical capacitors, each with a capacitance of $2.2 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, have their outer cylinders placed in contact while their inner cylinders are joined via a wire. A battery of emf $10 \text{ V}$ is connected as depicted in the figure. Determine the total charge delivered by the battery to the inner cylinders.
questions.solution:
From the given figure and description, the inner cylinders of the two capacitors are connected together via a wire, and their outer cylinders are in contact with each other. This means the two cylindrical capacitors are connected in parallel across the battery.

The equivalent capacitance $C_{eq}$ of two capacitors in parallel is the sum of their individual capacitances:
$C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2$

Given that each capacitor has a capacitance of $2.2 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$:
$C_{eq} = 2.2 \text{ }\mu\text{F} + 2.2 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 4.4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The battery has an emf of $V = 10 \text{ V}$. The total charge $Q$ delivered by the battery to the inner cylinders is given by:
$Q = C_{eq} \times V$

Substituting the values:
$Q = 4.4 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 10 \text{ V} = 44 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the assembly shown in the figure, each capacitor possesses a capacitance of $2 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Determine the equivalent capacitance between the points $A$ and $B$. Additionally, if a battery of emf $60$ volts is connected across $A$ and $B$, compute the potential difference that appears on the individual capacitors.
questions.solution:
The given circuit consists of three parallel branches between points A and B, where each branch contains three capacitors connected in series.

Let the capacitance of each individual capacitor be $C = 2 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

For a single branch, the equivalent capacitance $C_{\text{branch}}$ of the three capacitors in series is calculated as:

$\dfrac{1}{C_{\text{branch}}} = \dfrac{1}{C} + \dfrac{1}{C} + \dfrac{1}{C} = \dfrac{3}{C}$

$C_{\text{branch}} = \dfrac{C}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The total equivalent capacitance $C_{AB}$ of the three parallel branches is the sum of their individual capacitances:

$C_{AB} = C_{\text{branch}} + C_{\text{branch}} + C_{\text{branch}} = 3 \times \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right) = 2 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

When a battery of $60 \text{ V}$ is connected across A and B, the potential difference across each parallel branch is $60 \text{ V}$.

Since each branch consists of three identical capacitors in series, the total potential difference of $60 \text{ V}$ is divided equally among them.

The potential difference $V_c$ across each individual capacitor is:

$V_c = \dfrac{60}{3} = 20 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a circuit, a $50\text{ V}$ battery is connected across points A and B, with the positive terminal at A. An upper branch consists of a $4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and an $8\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor in series, with point C situated between them. A lower branch consists of a $3\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and a $6\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor in series, with point D situated between them. The $4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $3\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors are connected to A, while the $8\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $6\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors are connected to B. If an additional capacitor is connected between points C and D, what charge will appear on this capacitor?
questions.solution:
The circuit can be analyzed by finding the potentials at points C and D. Let the potential at B be $0\text{ V}$, then the potential at A is $50\text{ V}$.

In the upper branch, the $4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $8\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors are in series. The equivalent capacitance is:
$C_{upper} = \dfrac{4 \times 8}{4 + 8} = \dfrac{32}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3}\text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The charge on the upper branch is:
$Q_{upper} = C_{upper} \times V = \dfrac{8}{3} \times 50 = \dfrac{400}{3}\text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The potential difference across the $4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:
$V_A - V_C = \dfrac{Q_{upper}}{4\text{ }\mu\text{F}} = \dfrac{400/3}{4} = \dfrac{100}{3}\text{ V}$

In the lower branch, the $3\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $6\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors are in series. The equivalent capacitance is:
$C_{lower} = \dfrac{3 \times 6}{3 + 6} = \dfrac{18}{9} = 2\text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The charge on the lower branch is:
$Q_{lower} = C_{lower} \times V = 2 \times 50 = 100\text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The potential difference across the $3\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:
$V_A - V_D = \dfrac{Q_{lower}}{3\text{ }\mu\text{F}} = \dfrac{100}{3}\text{ V}$

Since $V_A - V_C = V_A - V_D = \dfrac{100}{3}\text{ V}$, we have $V_C = V_D$.

Alternatively, the circuit forms a Wheatstone bridge. The ratio of capacitances in the upper branch is $\dfrac{4}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$, and in the lower branch is $\dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$. Since the ratios are equal, the bridge is balanced, meaning points C and D are at the same potential.

Because the potential difference between C and D is zero ($V_C - V_D = 0$), no charge will appear on any additional capacitor connected between these two points. Thus, the charge is $0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An equivalent capacitor of $10 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ that can be connected across a $200 \text{ V}$ battery is required to be constructed. Capacitors of capacitance $10 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ are available, but they can withstand only $50 \text{ V}$. Which of the following combinations yields the desired result?
options: 2 parallel branches, each containing 4 capacitors in series
4 parallel branches, each containing 2 capacitors in series
4 parallel branches, each containing 4 capacitors in series
questions.solution:
Let $n$ be the number of capacitors connected in series in each branch.

The voltage across each capacitor in the series combination is $\dfrac{V}{n}$.

Since the total voltage is $200 \text{ V}$ and each capacitor can withstand a maximum of $50 \text{ V}$, we have:

$\dfrac{200}{n} \le 50 \Rightarrow n \ge 4$

Taking the minimum number of capacitors in series, $n = 4$.

The equivalent capacitance of one such branch containing 4 capacitors in series is:

$C_s = \dfrac{C}{n} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

Let $m$ be the number of such branches connected in parallel to achieve the desired total capacitance.

The total equivalent capacitance is:

$C_{eq} = m \times C_s$

Given that the required equivalent capacitance is $10 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, we get:

$m \times 2.5 = 10 \Rightarrow m = 4$

Thus, 4 parallel branches, each containing 4 capacitors in series, are required.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the equivalent capacitance between the points $a$ and $b$ for the system presented in the figure.
questions.solution:
The given circuit can be simplified by identifying the parallel and series combinations of the capacitors.

The circuit consists of three parallel branches connected across the terminals $a$ and $b$:

1. The upper branch has capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ connected in series. The equivalent capacitance of this branch is $C_{upper} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.

2. The lower branch also has capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ connected in series. The equivalent capacitance of this branch is $C_{lower} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.

3. The middle branch has a single capacitor $C_3$ connected directly between $a$ and $b$.

Since these three branches are connected in parallel, the total equivalent capacitance $C_{ab}$ is the sum of their individual capacitances:

$C_{ab} = C_{upper} + C_3 + C_{lower}$

$C_{ab} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} + C_3 + \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$

$C_{ab} = C_3 + \dfrac{2 C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$

Answer: $C_3 + \dfrac{2 C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor consists of a flat plate of area $A$ and another plate with a stair-like geometry, as depicted in the figure. Each stair has a width $a$ and a height $b$. Determine the capacitance of this assembly.
questions.solution:
The given capacitor can be considered as a parallel combination of three smaller capacitors.

The total area of the flat plate is $A$ and its total length is $3a$. Therefore, the area of each stair section of width $a$ is $\dfrac{A}{3}$.

The distances between the plates for the three sections are $d$, $d+b$, and $d+2b$.

The capacitances of the three sections are:
$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 (A/3)}{d}$
$C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 (A/3)}{d+b}$
$C_3 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 (A/3)}{d+2b}$

Since the three capacitors are connected in parallel, the equivalent capacitance is:
$C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2 + C_3$

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3} \left( \dfrac{1}{d} + \dfrac{1}{d+b} + \dfrac{1}{d+2b} \right)$

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3} \left[ \dfrac{(d+b)(d+2b) + d(d+2b) + d(d+b)}{d(d+b)(d+2b)} \right]$

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3} \left[ \dfrac{(d^2 + 3bd + 2b^2) + (d^2 + 2bd) + (d^2 + bd)}{d(d+b)(d+2b)} \right]$

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A (3d^2 + 6bd + 2b^2)}{3d(d+b)(d+2b)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical capacitor is built using two coaxial cylinders, each having a length of $10 \text{ cm}$, and with radii of $2 \text{ mm}$ and $4 \text{ mm}$. Determine the capacitance.
questions.solution:
The capacitance of a cylindrical capacitor is given by:

$C = \dfrac{2 \pi \epsilon_0 L}{\ln\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)}$

Given $L = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $a = 2 \text{ mm}$, and $b = 4 \text{ mm}$.

Substituting the values:

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{\left(18 \times 10^9\right) \ln\left(\dfrac{4}{2}\right)}$

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{18 \times 10^9 \times \ln 2}$

Using $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$:

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{18 \times 10^9 \times 0.693}$

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{12.474 \times 10^9}$

$C \approx 8.01 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 8 \text{ pF}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical capacitor is constructed using two coaxial cylinders, each having a length of $10 \text{ cm}$, and with radii of $4 \text{ mm}$ and $8 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the capacitance.
questions.solution:
The capacitance of a cylindrical capacitor is given by the formula:

$C = \dfrac{2 \pi \epsilon_0 L}{\ln(b/a)}$

Given:
Length $L = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
Inner radius $a = 4 \text{ mm}$
Outer radius $b = 8 \text{ mm}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$C = \dfrac{2 \pi \epsilon_0 (0.1)}{\ln(8/4)}$

Using $\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, we have $2 \pi \epsilon_0 = \dfrac{1}{18 \times 10^9} \text{ F/m}$.

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{18 \times 10^9 \times \ln 2}$

Since $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$:

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{18 \times 10^9 \times 0.693}$

$C = \dfrac{0.1}{12.474 \times 10^9} \approx 8.01 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$

$C \approx 8 \text{ pF}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ pF}$ capacitor is charged to a potential difference of $24 \text{ V}$. Subsequently, it is connected to an uncharged capacitor of $20 \text{ pF}$. Determine the new potential difference across the $100 \text{ pF}$ capacitor.
questions.solution:
The initial charge on the first capacitor is given by $Q = C_1 V_1$.

Substituting the given values, $Q = 100 \times 24 = 2400 \text{ pC}$.

When this capacitor is connected to an uncharged capacitor of capacitance $C_2 = 20 \text{ pF}$, the total charge remains conserved and is shared between them until they reach a common potential $V$.

The equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination is $C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2 = 100 + 20 = 120 \text{ pF}$.

The common potential difference $V$ across both capacitors is given by $V = \dfrac{Q}{C_{eq}}$.

Substituting the values, $V = \dfrac{2400}{120} = 20 \text{ V}$.

Therefore, the new potential difference across the $100 \text{ pF}$ capacitor is $20 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown in the figure, each capacitor has a capacitance of $5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and the battery has an emf of $50 \text{ V}$. Determine the amount of charge that will flow through $AB$ when the switch $S$ is closed.
questions.solution:
Let the capacitance of each capacitor be $C = 5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and the voltage of the battery be $V = 50 \text{ V}$.

Before the switch $S$ is closed, the circuit consists of two capacitors in parallel, which are in series with a third capacitor.
The equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination is:
$C_{p} = C + C = 5.0 + 5.0 = 10.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

This combination is in series with the third capacitor $C$. The initial equivalent capacitance of the entire circuit is:
$C_{initial} = \dfrac{C_{p} \times C}{C_{p} + C} = \dfrac{10.0 \times 5.0}{10.0 + 5.0} = \dfrac{50}{15} = \dfrac{10}{3} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The initial charge supplied by the battery is:
$Q_{initial} = C_{initial} \times V = \dfrac{10}{3} \times 50 = \dfrac{500}{3} \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

When the switch $S$ is closed, it short-circuits the third capacitor. The circuit now consists only of the parallel combination of the first two capacitors connected directly across the battery.
The final equivalent capacitance is:
$C_{final} = C_{p} = 10.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The final charge supplied by the battery is:
$Q_{final} = C_{final} \times V = 10.0 \times 50 = 500 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The amount of charge that flows through the wire $AB$ is the additional charge drawn from the battery:
$\Delta Q = Q_{final} - Q_{initial} = 500 - \dfrac{500}{3} = \dfrac{1000}{3} \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

$\Delta Q \approx 333.3 \text{ }\mu\text{C} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor is constructed from circular discs, each with a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$. Determine the capacitance if the separation between the plates is $1.0 \text{ mm}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Radius of circular disc, $r = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$
Separation between plates, $d = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
Permittivity of free space, $\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

Area of the plate, $A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 25\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

Capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is given by:
$C = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$

Substituting the values:
$C = \dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 25\pi \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}}$

$C = 8.85 \times 25 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-13}$

$C = 694.7 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}$

$C \approx 6.95 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor with a plate area of $25 \text{ cm}^2$ and a separation of $1.00 \text{ mm}$ is connected to a $6.0 \text{ V}$ battery. Determine the charge that has flown through the battery and the work done by the battery during this process.
questions.solution:
The capacitance of the parallel-plate capacitor is given by:

$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$

Substituting the given values:

$C = \dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{-4}}{1.00 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.2125 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F}$

The charge that flows through the battery is:

$Q = CV = 2.2125 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.0 = 1.3275 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C} \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C}$

The work done by the battery is the product of the charge that flows through it and its electromotive force (voltage):

$W = QV = 1.3275 \times 10^{-10} \times 6.0 = 7.965 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J} \approx 8.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a particle $P$ carries a charge of $-0.01 \text{ } \mu\text{C}$ and has a mass of $10 \text{ mg}$. Each capacitor plate possesses a one-sided surface area of $100 \text{ cm}^2$. Determine the potential difference $V$ that must be applied across the combination to maintain the particle $P$ in equilibrium.
questions.solution:
For the particle to be in equilibrium, the upward electric force must balance the downward gravitational force:

$|q|E = mg$

Since the two identical capacitors are connected in series, the potential difference across each capacitor is $V' = \dfrac{V}{2}$.

The electric field between the plates of the top capacitor is $E = \dfrac{V'}{d} = \dfrac{V}{2d}$.

Substituting this into the equilibrium equation:

$|q|\dfrac{V}{2d} = mg \Rightarrow V = \dfrac{2dmg}{|q|}$

The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is $C = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$, which gives $d = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{C}$.

Substituting $d$ into the expression for $V$:

$V = \dfrac{2mg}{|q|} \left( \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{C} \right)$

Substituting the given values ($m = 10^{-5} \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$, $|q| = 10^{-8} \text{ C}$, $\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$, $A = 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$, $C = 4 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F}$):

$V = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-5} \times 9.8 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-8} \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}$

$V = \dfrac{19.6 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-19}}{4 \times 10^{-16}}$

$V = 4.9 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

$V = 43.365 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 43 \text{ mV}$

Answer: $43 \text{ mV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three capacitors with capacitances of $20 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $30 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, and $40 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ are connected in series across a $12 \text{ V}$ battery. Determine the charge acquired by each capacitor and the work done by the battery during the charging process.
questions.solution:
The equivalent capacitance $C_{eq}$ for capacitors connected in series is given by:

$\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2} + \dfrac{1}{C_3}$

$\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{20} + \dfrac{1}{30} + \dfrac{1}{40} = \dfrac{6 + 4 + 3}{120} = \dfrac{13}{120} \text{ }\mu\text{F}^{-1}$

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{120}{13} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

In a series circuit, the charge $Q$ on each capacitor is the same and is equal to the total charge supplied by the battery:

$Q = C_{eq}V = \left(\dfrac{120}{13} \text{ }\mu\text{F}\right) \times 12 \text{ V} = \dfrac{1440}{13} \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The work done by the battery during the charging process is given by $W = QV$:

$W = \left(\dfrac{1440}{13} \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}\right) \times 12 \text{ V} = \dfrac{17280}{13} \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}$

$W \approx 1329.23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} = 1.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The two capacitors depicted in the figure consist of square plates of edge $a$. Their plate separations are $d_1$ and $d_2$ respectively. A potential difference $V$ is applied across the points $a$ and $b$. An electron is projected along the central line between the plates of the upper capacitor. At what minimum speed must the electron be projected to ensure it does not collide with either plate? Account for electric forces only.
questions.solution:
The two capacitors are connected in series. Let their capacitances be $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 a^2}{d_1}$ and $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 a^2}{d_2}$.

The potential difference across the upper capacitor is given by $V_1 = V \dfrac{C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.

Substituting the expressions for capacitance, we get:
$V_1 = V \dfrac{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 a^2}{d_2}}{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 a^2}{d_1} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 a^2}{d_2}} = V \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2}$

The electric field between the plates of the upper capacitor is:
$E_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{d_1} = \dfrac{V}{d_1 + d_2}$

The acceleration of the electron in the vertical direction is:
$a_y = \dfrac{e E_1}{m} = \dfrac{e V}{m (d_1 + d_2)}$

The time taken by the electron to cross the plates of length $a$ with horizontal speed $u$ is $t = \dfrac{a}{u}$.

For the electron to just avoid colliding with the plates, its maximum vertical deflection should be $y = \dfrac{d_1}{2}$.

Using the equation of motion $y = \dfrac{1}{2} a_y t^2$, we have:
$\dfrac{d_1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{e V}{m (d_1 + d_2)} \right) \left( \dfrac{a}{u} \right)^2$

$d_1 = \dfrac{e V a^2}{m u^2 (d_1 + d_2)}$

$u^2 = \dfrac{e V a^2}{m d_1 (d_1 + d_2)}$

$u = \sqrt{\dfrac{e V a^2}{m d_1 (d_1 + d_2)}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the capacitance between points $A$ and $B$ of a circuit constructed with five capacitors. Point $A$ is connected to a $1\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor and a $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor. The other terminal of the $1\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is connected to a node $C$, and the other terminal of the $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is connected to a node $D$. A $3\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor connects node $C$ to point $B$, and a $6\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor connects node $D$ to point $B$. A $5\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is placed between nodes $C$ and $D$.
questions.solution:
The given circuit forms a Wheatstone bridge with the following capacitors:

$C_1 = 1 \mu\text{F}$ (between A and C)
$C_2 = 2 \mu\text{F}$ (between A and D)
$C_3 = 3 \mu\text{F}$ (between C and B)
$C_4 = 6 \mu\text{F}$ (between D and B)
$C_5 = 5 \mu\text{F}$ (between C and D)

Checking the balance condition for the Wheatstone bridge:
$\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\dfrac{C_3}{C_4} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Since $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{C_3}{C_4}$, the bridge is balanced. Therefore, the potential at node C is equal to the potential at node D ($V_C = V_D$). No charge will flow through the $5 \mu\text{F}$ capacitor, and it can be removed from the circuit.

The circuit now simplifies to two parallel branches:
Upper branch (series combination of $C_1$ and $C_3$):
$C_{upper} = \dfrac{C_1 C_3}{C_1 + C_3} = \dfrac{1 \times 3}{1 + 3} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75 \mu\text{F}$

Lower branch (series combination of $C_2$ and $C_4$):
$C_{lower} = \dfrac{C_2 C_4}{C_2 + C_4} = \dfrac{2 \times 6}{2 + 6} = \dfrac{12}{8} = 1.5 \mu\text{F}$

The equivalent capacitance between points A and B is the sum of the capacitances of these parallel branches:
$C_{AB} = C_{upper} + C_{lower} = 0.75 + 1.5 = 2.25 \mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the equivalent capacitance between the points $A$ and $B$ for the circuit labeled (a) in the assembly.
questions.solution:
The given circuit (a) is a Wheatstone bridge.

Let the capacitors be $C_1 = 1\ \mu\text{F}$, $C_2 = 2\ \mu\text{F}$, $C_3 = 3\ \mu\text{F}$, $C_4 = 6\ \mu\text{F}$, and the central capacitor be $C_5 = 5\ \mu\text{F}$.

Checking the balance condition of the Wheatstone bridge:

$\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

$\dfrac{C_3}{C_4} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Since $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{C_3}{C_4}$, the bridge is balanced. Therefore, no charge will flow through the central capacitor $C_5$, and it can be removed from the circuit.

The circuit now simplifies to two parallel branches. The top branch has $C_1$ and $C_3$ in series, and the bottom branch has $C_2$ and $C_4$ in series.

Equivalent capacitance of the top branch:

$C_{top} = \dfrac{C_1 C_3}{C_1 + C_3} = \dfrac{1 \times 3}{1 + 3} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75\ \mu\text{F}$

Equivalent capacitance of the bottom branch:

$C_{bottom} = \dfrac{C_2 C_4}{C_2 + C_4} = \dfrac{2 \times 6}{2 + 6} = \dfrac{12}{8} = 1.5\ \mu\text{F}$

The total equivalent capacitance between A and B is the sum of the capacitances of these parallel branches:

$C_{AB} = C_{top} + C_{bottom} = 0.75 + 1.5 = 2.25\ \mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Compute the equivalent capacitance between terminals $A$ and $B$ for the following network: A $1\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor and a $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor are joined at terminal $A$. Their respective other ends connect to intermediate junctions $X$ and $Y$. From junction $X$, a $3\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor connects to terminal $B$. From junction $Y$, a $6\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor connects to terminal $B$. Finally, a $5\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor bridges junctions $X$ and $Y$.
questions.solution:
The given circuit represents a Wheatstone bridge.

The ratio of capacitances in the arms connected to terminal A is $\dfrac{C_{AX}}{C_{AY}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

The ratio of capacitances in the arms connected to terminal B is $\dfrac{C_{XB}}{C_{YB}} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

Since $\dfrac{C_{AX}}{C_{AY}} = \dfrac{C_{XB}}{C_{YB}}$, the Wheatstone bridge is balanced.

Therefore, the potential at junction X is equal to the potential at junction Y, and no charge flows through the $5\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor. It can be removed from the circuit.

The equivalent capacitance of the upper branch ($1\ \mu\text{F}$ and $3\ \mu\text{F}$ in series) is:
$C_{upper} = \dfrac{1 \times 3}{1 + 3} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75\ \mu\text{F}$

The equivalent capacitance of the lower branch ($2\ \mu\text{F}$ and $6\ \mu\text{F}$ in series) is:
$C_{lower} = \dfrac{2 \times 6}{2 + 6} = \dfrac{12}{8} = 1.5\ \mu\text{F}$

The total equivalent capacitance between A and B is the parallel combination of $C_{upper}$ and $C_{lower}$:
$C_{eq} = C_{upper} + C_{lower} = 0.75 + 1.5 = 2.25\ \mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the potential difference $V_a - V_b$ between the points $a$ and $b$ for the circuit labeled (a) in the given figure.
questions.solution:
In the circuit labeled (a), let the potential at the bottom wire be $V_b = 0$.

The $12 \text{ V}$ battery fixes the potential at the node directly above it to $12 \text{ V}$.

Let the potential at the node between the $12 \text{ V}$ battery and node $a$ be $x$.

Applying the principle of charge conservation at node $x$ yields $2(x - 12) + 4(x - 0) + 2(x - V_a) = 0$, which simplifies to $8x - 24 - 2V_a = 0$ or $4x - V_a = 12$.

Applying charge conservation at node $a$ gives $2(V_a - x) + 4(V_a - 0) = 0$, resulting in $6V_a - 2x = 0$, or $x = 3V_a$.

Substituting $x = 3V_a$ into the first equation gives $4(3V_a) - V_a = 12$, so $11V_a = 12$ and $V_a = \dfrac{12}{11} \text{ V}$.

Therefore, the potential difference is $V_a - V_b = \dfrac{12}{11} - 0 = \dfrac{12}{11} \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two conducting spheres having radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ are placed widely apart from one another. Determine their individual capacitances. Furthermore, if a metal wire connects the spheres, what is the capacitance of the combination? (Consider the setup in terms of series-parallel connections.)
questions.solution:
The capacitance of an isolated spherical conductor of radius $R$ is given by $C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R$.

For the two spheres of radii $R_1$ and $R_2$, their individual capacitances are $C_1 = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_1$ and $C_2 = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_2$.

When the two spheres are connected by a metal wire, they acquire the same potential. The second plate for each spherical capacitor is considered to be at infinity, which is at a common zero potential. This means the two capacitors are effectively connected in parallel.

The equivalent capacitance of the combination is the sum of their individual capacitances:

$C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2 = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_1 + 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_2 = 4\pi\varepsilon_0(R_1 + R_2)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the potential difference $V_a - V_b$ between nodes $a$ and $b$ for the circuit labeled (c) in the provided figure.
questions.solution:
In the circuit labeled (c), let the potential at the bottom middle node $b$ be $V_b = 0$.

The left battery has an electromotive force of $2 \text{ V}$ with its positive terminal facing right. The potential at the node between the left battery and the left $2 \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is $V_a - 2$.

The right battery also has an electromotive force of $2 \text{ V}$ with its positive terminal facing right (indicated by the longer line). The potential at the node between the right battery and the right $2 \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is $V_a + 2$.

Let the capacitance of the middle capacitor be $C$. Applying charge conservation for the isolated plates connected to node $b$:

$2(V_b - (V_a - 2)) + 2(V_b - (V_a + 2)) + C(V_b - V_a) = 0$

Substituting $V_b = 0$ yields $2(-V_a + 2) + 2(-V_a - 2) + C(-V_a) = 0$.

$-2V_a + 4 - 2V_a - 4 - CV_a = 0$

$-(4 + C)V_a = 0 \implies V_a = 0$.

Therefore, the potential difference is $V_a - V_b = 0 - 0 = 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Evaluate the potential difference $V_a - V_b$ between points $a$ and $b$ for the circuit designated as (d) in the figure.
questions.solution:
In the circuit labeled (d), let the potential at node $a$ be $V_a = 0$.

The circuit consists of three parallel branches connected between nodes $a$ and $b$. Each branch contains a battery and a capacitor in series.

For all three batteries, the positive terminal (longer line) is oriented towards the right.

The potentials at the intermediate nodes between the batteries and capacitors are $6 \text{ V}$ (top branch), $12 \text{ V}$ (middle branch), and $24 \text{ V}$ (bottom branch).

Applying the principle of charge conservation for the isolated plates connected to node $b$:

$4(V_b - 6) + 2(V_b - 12) + 1(V_b - 24) = 0$

Expanding the equation gives $4V_b - 24 + 2V_b - 24 + V_b - 24 = 0$.

$7V_b - 72 = 0 \implies V_b = \dfrac{72}{7} \approx 10.28 \text{ V}$.

The potential difference is $V_a - V_b = 0 - 10.28 = -10.28 \text{ V}$, which corresponds to $-10.3 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the equivalent capacitances between the specified points for the combinations illustrated in the figure.
questions.solution:
For circuit (a):
Let the input node be at potential $V$ and the output node be at $0$. Let the potentials at the top and bottom junctions be $x$ and $y$ respectively.
Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top junction:
$(x - V) \times 1 + (x - 0) \times 3 + (x - y) \times 4 = 0 \Rightarrow 8x - 4y = V$
Applying KCL at the bottom junction:
$(y - V) \times 3 + (y - 0) \times 1 + (y - x) \times 4 = 0 \Rightarrow -4x + 8y = 3V$
Solving these two equations, we get $x = \dfrac{5V}{12}$ and $y = \dfrac{7V}{12}$.
The total charge $Q$ supplied by the source is the sum of charges on the input capacitors:
$Q = (V - x) \times 1 + (V - y) \times 3 = \left(V - \dfrac{5V}{12}\right) \times 1 + \left(V - \dfrac{7V}{12}\right) \times 3 = \dfrac{7V}{12} + \dfrac{15V}{12} = \dfrac{22V}{12} = \dfrac{11V}{6}$
The equivalent capacitance is $C_{eq} = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{11}{6} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

For circuit (b):
Let the input be at $V$ and output at $0$. Let the potentials at the top, middle, and bottom junctions be $V_C$, $V_D$, and $V_E$ respectively.
Applying KCL at the three junctions:
Top: $(V_C - V) \times 1 + V_C \times 3 + (V_C - V_D) \times 4 = 0 \Rightarrow 8V_C - 4V_D = V$
Middle: $(V_D - V) \times 2 + V_D \times 2 + (V_D - V_C) \times 4 + (V_D - V_E) \times 4 = 0 \Rightarrow 12V_D - 4V_C - 4V_E = 2V$
Bottom: $(V_E - V) \times 3 + V_E \times 1 + (V_E - V_D) \times 4 = 0 \Rightarrow 8V_E - 4V_D = 3V$
Adding the top and bottom equations gives $8(V_C + V_E) - 8V_D = 4V \Rightarrow V_C + V_E = V_D + \dfrac{V}{2}$.
Substituting this into the middle equation gives $12V_D - 4\left(V_D + \dfrac{V}{2}\right) = 2V \Rightarrow 8V_D = 4V \Rightarrow V_D = \dfrac{V}{2}$.
Using $V_D = \dfrac{V}{2}$ in the top and bottom equations yields $V_C = \dfrac{3V}{8}$ and $V_E = \dfrac{5V}{8}$.
Total charge $Q = (V - V_C) \times 1 + (V - V_D) \times 2 + (V - V_E) \times 3 = \dfrac{5V}{8} + V + \dfrac{9V}{8} = \dfrac{22V}{8} = \dfrac{11V}{4}$.
The equivalent capacitance is $C_{eq} = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{11}{4} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

For circuit (c):
The upper part of the circuit forms a Wheatstone bridge. The ratio of capacitances in the top branch is $\dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and in the middle branch is $\dfrac{4}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.
Since the bridge is balanced, no charge flows through the $5 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor, and it can be removed.
The equivalent capacitance of the bridge is $\dfrac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} + \dfrac{4 \times 8}{4 + 8} = \dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{8}{3} = 4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.
This is in parallel with the bottom $4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor, so $C_{eq} = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

For circuit (d):
The circuit consists of four parallel branches. The ratio of capacitances in each branch is $\dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$, $\dfrac{4}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$, $\dfrac{4}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$, and $\dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.
Because the ratios are identical, the midpoints of all four branches are at the same potential. Thus, the three $6 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitors have no potential difference across them and can be removed.
The equivalent capacitance is the sum of the series combinations of each branch:
$C_{eq} = \dfrac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} + \dfrac{4 \times 8}{4 + 8} + \dfrac{4 \times 8}{4 + 8} + \dfrac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} = \dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{8}{3} + \dfrac{8}{3} + \dfrac{4}{3} = \dfrac{24}{3} = 8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

Answer: (a) $\dfrac{11}{6} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, (b) $\dfrac{11}{4} \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, (c) $8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, (d) $8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Calculate the potential difference $V_a - V_b$ between points $a$ and $b$ for the circuit labeled (b) as shown in the figure.
questions.solution:
The circuit labeled (b) is a single closed loop containing two capacitors and two batteries.

The equivalent capacitance $C_{eq}$ of the $2 \mu\text{F}$ and $4 \mu\text{F}$ capacitors in series is $C_{eq} = \dfrac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} = \dfrac{4}{3} \mu\text{F}$.

Traversing the loop in the clockwise direction, the $12 \text{ V}$ battery acts to drive current clockwise, while the $24 \text{ V}$ battery opposes it. The net electromotive force is $\mathcal{E}_{net} = 24 - 12 = 12 \text{ V}$ in the counter-clockwise direction.

The steady-state charge $Q$ on the equivalent capacitor is $Q = C_{eq} \mathcal{E}_{net} = \left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)(12) = 16 \mu\text{C}$.

This counter-clockwise flow of charge polarizes the capacitors such that the bottom plate of the $2 \mu\text{F}$ capacitor (at node $b$) is positive and the top plate (at node $a$) is negative.

The potential difference across the $2 \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is $V_c = \dfrac{Q}{C} = \dfrac{16}{2} = 8 \text{ V}$.

Since the plate at node $b$ is at a higher potential, $V_b - V_a = 8 \text{ V}$, which implies $V_a - V_b = -8 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the equivalent capacitance between the terminals $A$ and $B$ for the capacitor network illustrated in the diagram.
questions.solution:
Starting from the left end of the given capacitor network, the leftmost vertical capacitor ($2\ \mu\text{F}$) and the adjacent horizontal capacitor ($2\ \mu\text{F}$) are connected in series.

Their equivalent capacitance is:

$C_1 = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{2 + 2} = 1\ \mu\text{F}$

This equivalent capacitor $C_1$ is in parallel with the first vertical $1\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor.

Their equivalent capacitance is:

$C_2 = 1 + 1 = 2\ \mu\text{F}$

Now, $C_2$ is in series with the next horizontal $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor.

Their equivalent capacitance is:

$C_3 = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{2 + 2} = 1\ \mu\text{F}$

This equivalent capacitor $C_3$ is in parallel with the second vertical $1\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor.

Their equivalent capacitance is:

$C_4 = 1 + 1 = 2\ \mu\text{F}$

Finally, $C_4$ is in series with the rightmost horizontal $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor connected to terminal A.

The equivalent capacitance between terminals A and B is:

$C_{AB} = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{2 + 2} = 1\ \mu\text{F}$

Answer: $1\ \mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming the potential at point B is zero, determine the potentials at points C and D in the circuit shown in the figure.
options: $\dfrac{50}{3}\text{ V}$ at C and $\dfrac{25}{3}\text{ V}$ at D
questions.solution:
Given the potential at point B is $V_B = 0\text{ V}$.

The battery is connected across points A and B with its positive terminal (longer line) towards A and negative terminal (shorter line) towards B. Thus, the potential at point A is $V_A = 50\text{ V}$.

In the upper branch, the $4\ \mu\text{F}$ and $8\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitors are connected in series across A and B. The potential difference across the $8\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is $V_C - V_B$.

Using the voltage divider rule for capacitors in series ($V_1 = V \times \dfrac{C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$), the voltage across the $8\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:
$V_C - V_B = (V_A - V_B) \times \dfrac{4}{4 + 8}$

$V_C - 0 = 50 \times \dfrac{4}{12} = \dfrac{50}{3}\text{ V}$

In the lower branch, the $3\ \mu\text{F}$ and $6\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitors are connected in series across A and B. The potential difference across the $6\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is $V_D - V_B$.

Similarly, the voltage across the $6\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:
$V_D - V_B = (V_A - V_B) \times \dfrac{3}{3 + 6}$

$V_D - 0 = 50 \times \dfrac{3}{9} = \dfrac{50}{3}\text{ V}$

Therefore, the potentials at points C and D are both $\dfrac{50}{3}\text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the equivalent capacitance between points $A$ and $B$ for the infinite ladder circuit illustrated in the figure.
questions.solution:
Let the equivalent capacitance between points $A$ and $B$ be $C_{eq}$.

Because the ladder is infinite, the circuit to the right of the first $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is identical to the entire circuit and thus has an equivalent capacitance of $C_{eq}$.

The circuit can be simplified to a $1\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor in parallel with the series combination of a $2\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $C_{eq}$.

The equivalent capacitance is given by:
$C_{eq} = 1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot C_{eq}}{2 + C_{eq}}$

Multiplying both sides by $(2 + C_{eq})$:
$C_{eq}(2 + C_{eq}) = 1(2 + C_{eq}) + 2C_{eq}$

$2C_{eq} + C_{eq}^2 = 2 + 3C_{eq}$

$C_{eq}^2 - C_{eq} - 2 = 0$

Factoring the quadratic equation:
$(C_{eq} - 2)(C_{eq} + 1) = 0$

Since capacitance must be positive, we discard $C_{eq} = -1\ \mu\text{F}$.

Therefore, $C_{eq} = 2\ \mu\text{F}$.

Answer: $2\ \mu\text{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a finite ladder is assembled by connecting several sections of $2\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor combinations. The ladder is terminated by a capacitor of capacitance $C$. Determine the value that should be chosen for $C$ so that the equivalent capacitance of the ladder between points $A$ and $B$ remains independent of the number of sections.
questions.solution:
For the equivalent capacitance of the ladder between points A and B to be independent of the number of sections, the equivalent capacitance of the entire ladder must be equal to the terminating capacitance $C$.

Let the equivalent capacitance of the ladder be $C_{eq}$. By observing the repeating unit of the circuit from the terminals A and B, the ladder consists of a $2\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor in parallel with the series combination of a $4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and the rest of the ladder.

Thus, the equivalent capacitance is given by:

$C_{eq} = 2 + \dfrac{4 \times C_{eq}}{4 + C_{eq}}$

Since the equivalent capacitance is independent of the number of sections, we must have $C_{eq} = C$. Substituting this into the equation:

$C = 2 + \dfrac{4C}{4 + C}$

Multiplying both sides by $(4 + C)$:

$C(4 + C) = 2(4 + C) + 4C$

$4C + C^2 = 8 + 2C + 4C$

$C^2 - 2C - 8 = 0$

Factorizing the quadratic equation:

$(C - 4)(C + 2) = 0$

Since capacitance cannot be negative, we get $C = 4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of $1.2 	imes 10^{-3} 	ext{ F}$ has a charge of $+2.0 	imes 10^{-8} 	ext{ C}$ deposited on its positive plate and $-1.0 	imes 10^{-8} 	ext{ C}$ on its negative plate. Determine the potential difference that develops between the plates.
questions.solution:
When charges $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are given to the two plates of a parallel-plate capacitor, the charge that appears on the inner facing surfaces is $q = \dfrac{Q_1 - Q_2}{2}$.

Given $Q_1 = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}$ and $Q_2 = -1.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}$.

Substituting the values:

$q = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-8} - (-1.0 \times 10^{-8})}{2}$

$q = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-8}}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}$

The potential difference $V$ developed between the plates is given by $V = \dfrac{q}{C}$.

Given $C = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F}$.

$V = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$

$V = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One plate of a parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of $0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ is given a charge of $1 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$, while the other plate is given a charge of $2 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$. Determine the potential difference developed between the plates.
questions.solution:
Let the charges on the two plates be $Q_1$ and $Q_2$.

The charge appearing on the inner surfaces of the plates is given by $q = \dfrac{|Q_1 - Q_2|}{2}$.

Substituting the given values, $Q_1 = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ and $Q_2 = 2 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$:

$q = \dfrac{|1 - 2|}{2} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The potential difference $V$ between the plates is given by $V = \dfrac{q}{C}$.

Substituting $q = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ and $C = 0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$V = \dfrac{0.5}{0.1} = 5 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, each plate possesses a surface area of $(96/\varepsilon_0) \times 10^{-12} \text{ F-m}$ on a single side, and the gap separating consecutive plates is $4.0 \text{ mm}$. The connected battery provides an emf of $10 \text{ V}$. Calculate the magnitude of the charge delivered by the battery to each of the plates linked to it.
questions.solution:
Let the four plates be numbered 1, 2, 3, and 4 from left to right.

From the given circuit diagram:
Plate 1 is connected to the positive terminal of the battery, so its potential is $V_1 = 10 \text{ V}$.
Plate 3 is connected to the negative terminal of the battery, so its potential is $V_3 = 0 \text{ V}$.
Plates 2 and 4 are connected to each other by a wire, forming an isolated system. Let their common potential be $V_2 = V_4 = V'$.

The arrangement forms three identical capacitors between the consecutive pairs of plates: $C_{12}$, $C_{23}$, and $C_{34}$.
The capacitance of each capacitor is given by:
$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$
Given $\varepsilon_0 A = 96 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}\cdot\text{m}$ and $d = 4.0 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.
$C = \dfrac{96 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 24 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 24 \text{ nF}$

Since Plates 2 and 4 form an isolated system, the net charge on them is zero:
$q_2 + q_4 = 0$
The charge on Plate 2 is the sum of charges on its left and right surfaces:
$q_2 = C(V_2 - V_1) + C(V_2 - V_3) = C(V' - 10) + C(V' - 0)$
The charge on Plate 4 is on its left surface (facing Plate 3):
$q_4 = C(V_4 - V_3) = C(V' - 0)$

Substituting these into the charge conservation equation:
$C(V' - 10) + CV' + CV' = 0$
$3V' - 10 = 0 \implies V' = \dfrac{10}{3} \text{ V}$

Now, we calculate the charge on the plates connected to the battery (Plate 1 and Plate 3).
Charge on Plate 1:
$q_1 = C(V_1 - V_2) = C\left(10 - \dfrac{10}{3}\right) = C\left(\dfrac{20}{3}\right)$
$q_1 = 24 \text{ nF} \times \dfrac{20}{3} \text{ V} = 160 \text{ nC} = 0.16 \, \mu\text{C}$

Charge on Plate 3:
$q_3 = C(V_3 - V_2) + C(V_3 - V_4) = C\left(0 - \dfrac{10}{3}\right) + C\left(0 - \dfrac{10}{3}\right) = -C\left(\dfrac{20}{3}\right)$
$q_3 = -24 \text{ nF} \times \dfrac{20}{3} \text{ V} = -160 \text{ nC} = -0.16 \, \mu\text{C}$

The magnitude of the charge delivered by the battery to each of the plates linked to it is $0.16 \, \mu\text{C}$.

Answer: $0.16 \, \mu\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For the arrangement of plates depicted in the figure, the capacitance between adjacent plates is $50 \text{ nF}$. When a charge of $1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ is placed on the upper plate, what will be the potential difference between the upper plate and the middle plate?
questions.solution:
Let the three parallel plates be denoted as 1 (upper), 2 (middle), and 3 (lower).

The total charges on the isolated plates are given as:
$Q_1 = 1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$
$Q_2 = 0$
$Q_3 = 0$

For a system of parallel conducting plates, the charge appearing on the outermost surfaces is equal and is given by half the sum of the total charges on all plates:
$q_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3}{2} = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C} + 0 + 0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The charge on the upper surface of plate 1 is $q_{1a} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.
The charge on the lower surface of plate 1 is $q_{1b} = Q_1 - q_{1a} = 1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C} - 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.

The charge on the upper surface of plate 2 (facing plate 1) will be equal and opposite to $q_{1b}$:
$q_{2a} = -q_{1b} = -0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The upper and middle plates form a capacitor with charge $q = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on its inner surfaces.
Given the capacitance between adjacent plates is $C = 50 \text{ nF}$.

The potential difference $V$ between the upper and middle plates is:
$V = \dfrac{q}{C} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}}{50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ V} = 10 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The capacitance between adjacent plates in a three-plate arrangement is $50 ext{ nF}$. A charge of $1.0 ext{ }\mu\text{C}$ is placed on the middle plate. Calculate the potential difference developed between the upper and the middle plates.
questions.solution:
Let the charge given to the middle plate be $Q = 1.0 \mu\text{C}$.

By symmetry, the charge $Q$ distributes equally on the two faces of the middle plate. Therefore, the charge on the upper face of the middle plate is $q = \dfrac{Q}{2} = 0.5 \mu\text{C}$.

The upper plate and the middle plate act as a parallel plate capacitor with capacitance $C = 50 \text{ nF}$.

The potential difference $V$ between the upper and middle plates depends on the charge on their facing surfaces.

Using the relation $V = \dfrac{q}{C}$:

$V = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = 10 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three parallel plates are arranged one above the other, with a capacitance of $50 \text{ nF}$ between adjacent plates. When a charge of $1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ is deposited on the upper plate, what is the resulting potential difference between the middle plate and the lower plate?
questions.solution:
Let the three parallel plates be $P_1$ (upper), $P_2$ (middle), and $P_3$ (lower).

The total charge given to the system is $Q = 1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the upper plate, while the middle and lower plates are uncharged ($Q_2 = 0$, $Q_3 = 0$).

For a system of parallel conducting plates, the charge appearing on the outermost surfaces is half of the total charge of the system.
Charge on the outermost surfaces $= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{2} = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C} + 0 + 0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.

Thus, the top surface of $P_1$ has a charge of $0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.
Since the total charge on $P_1$ is $1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$, the charge on its bottom surface is:
$q_{1, \text{bottom}} = 1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{C} - 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.

Facing surfaces of adjacent conducting plates always carry equal and opposite charges.
Charge on the top surface of $P_2 = -0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.
Since $P_2$ is isolated and has zero net charge, the charge on its bottom surface is:
$q_{2, \text{bottom}} = 0 - (-0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}) = +0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.

Similarly, the charge on the top surface of $P_3$ will be $-0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$.

The middle and lower plates form a capacitor with capacitance $C = 50 \text{ nF}$ and charge $q = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{C} = 500 \text{ nC}$ on their facing surfaces.

The potential difference $V$ between the middle and lower plates is:
$V = \dfrac{q}{C} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C}}{50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}} = 10 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors of capacitances $20.0 \text{ pF}$ and $50.0 \text{ pF}$ are connected in series with a $6.00 \text{ V}$ battery. Determine the potential difference across the $20.0 \text{ pF}$ and $50.0 \text{ pF}$ capacitors respectively.
questions.solution:
Given $C_1 = 20.0 \text{ pF}$ and $C_2 = 50.0 \text{ pF}$ connected in series with $V = 6.00 \text{ V}$.

In a series combination, the charge $Q$ on each capacitor is the same.

The equivalent capacitance is $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \dfrac{20.0 \times 50.0}{20.0 + 50.0} = \dfrac{100}{7} \text{ pF}$.

Total charge $Q = C_{eq} V = \dfrac{100}{7} \times 6.00 = \dfrac{600}{7} \text{ pC}$.

Potential difference across the $20.0 \text{ pF}$ capacitor is $V_1 = \dfrac{Q}{C_1} = \dfrac{600/7}{20.0} = \dfrac{30}{7} \approx 4.29 \text{ V}$.

Potential difference across the $50.0 \text{ pF}$ capacitor is $V_2 = \dfrac{Q}{C_2} = \dfrac{600/7}{50.0} = \dfrac{12}{7} \approx 1.71 \text{ V}$.

Alternatively, using the voltage divider rule:

$V_1 = V \left( \dfrac{C_2}{C_1 + C_2} \right) = 6.00 \times \dfrac{50.0}{70.0} \approx 4.29 \text{ V}$

$V_2 = V \left( \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \right) = 6.00 \times \dfrac{20.0}{70.0} \approx 1.71 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors of capacitances $20.0 \text{ pF}$ and $50.0 \text{ pF}$ are connected in series with a $6.00 \text{ V}$ battery. Calculate the energy stored in the $20.0 \text{ pF}$ and $50.0 \text{ pF}$ capacitors respectively.
questions.solution:
Equivalent capacitance of the series combination is given by:

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \dfrac{20 \times 50}{20 + 50} = \dfrac{100}{7} \text{ pF}$

The charge on each capacitor in series is the same and is equal to:

$Q = C_{eq} V = \dfrac{100}{7} \times 6 = \dfrac{600}{7} \text{ pC}$

Energy stored in the $20.0 \text{ pF}$ capacitor is:

$U_1 = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C_1} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{600}{7}\right)^2}{2 \times 20} = \dfrac{360000}{49 \times 40} = \dfrac{9000}{49} \approx 184 \text{ pJ}$

Energy stored in the $50.0 \text{ pF}$ capacitor is:

$U_2 = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C_2} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{600}{7}\right)^2}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{360000}{49 \times 100} = \dfrac{3600}{49} \approx 73.5 \text{ pJ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors with capacitances of $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and $6.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ are connected in series across a $20 \text{ V}$ battery. Determine the energy supplied by the battery.
questions.solution:
The equivalent capacitance $C_{eq}$ of the two capacitors connected in series is given by:

$C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 6.0}{4.0 + 6.0} = 2.4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The total charge $Q$ drawn from the battery is:

$Q = C_{eq} V = 2.4 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 20 \text{ V} = 48 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The energy supplied by the battery is the work done in moving this charge across the potential difference $V$:

$E = Q V = 48 \text{ }\mu\text{C} \times 20 \text{ V} = 960 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown in the figure, every capacitor has a capacitance of $10 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$. The battery provides an emf of $100 \text{ V}$. Determine the energy stored in each of the four capacitors.
options: $16 \text{ mJ}$ in (a) and (d), $4 \text{ mJ}$ in (b) and (c)
$8 \text{ mJ}$ in (a) and (d), $2 \text{ mJ}$ in (b) and (c)
$2 \text{ mJ}$ in (a) and (d), $8 \text{ mJ}$ in (b) and (c)
$4 \text{ mJ}$ in (a) and (d), $4 \text{ mJ}$ in (b) and (c)
questions.solution:
From the given circuit, capacitors $b$ and $c$ are connected in parallel, and their combination is in series with capacitors $a$ and $d$.

The equivalent capacitance of $b$ and $c$ is:
$C_{bc} = C_b + C_c = 10\text{ }\mu\text{F} + 10\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 20\text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The total equivalent capacitance of the circuit $C_{eq}$ is:
$\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{C_a} + \dfrac{1}{C_{bc}} + \dfrac{1}{C_d}$

$\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{10} + \dfrac{1}{20} + \dfrac{1}{10} = \dfrac{5}{20}\text{ }\mu\text{F}^{-1}$

$C_{eq} = 4\text{ }\mu\text{F}$

The total charge supplied by the battery is:
$Q = C_{eq}V = 4\text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 100\text{ V} = 400\text{ }\mu\text{C}$

Since capacitors $a$ and $d$ are in series with the main circuit, the charge on each is:
$Q_a = Q_d = 400\text{ }\mu\text{C}$

Since capacitors $b$ and $c$ are in parallel and have equal capacitance, the total charge splits equally between them:
$Q_b = Q_c = \dfrac{400\text{ }\mu\text{C}}{2} = 200\text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The energy stored in a capacitor is given by $U = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C}$.

Energy stored in capacitors $a$ and $d$:
$U_a = U_d = \dfrac{(400 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{160000 \times 10^{-12}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 8 \times 10^{-3}\text{ J} = 8\text{ mJ}$

Energy stored in capacitors $b$ and $c$:
$U_b = U_c = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{40000 \times 10^{-12}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\text{ J} = 2\text{ mJ}$

Answer: $8 \text{ mJ}$ in (a) and (d), $2 \text{ mJ}$ in (b) and (c)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor holding $4.0 \text{ J}$ of stored energy is connected to an identical capacitor that has no electric field between its plates. Determine the total energy stored across the two capacitors.
questions.solution:
Let the capacitance of each capacitor be $C$ and the initial charge on the first capacitor be $Q$.

Initial energy of the first capacitor is $U_{i} = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C} = 4.0 \text{ J}$.

When it is connected to an identical uncharged capacitor, the total charge $Q$ is conserved.

The equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination becomes $C_{eq} = C + C = 2C$.

The final total energy stored in the system is $U_{f} = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C_{eq}} = \dfrac{Q^2}{2(2C)}$.

$U_{f} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{Q^2}{2C} \right) = \dfrac{1}{2} U_{i}$.

Substituting the given value, $U_{f} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \text{ J} = 2.0 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor having a capacitance of $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ is charged to a potential difference of $12 \text{ V}$. Subsequently, it is connected to an uncharged capacitor of capacitance $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ as shown in the figure. Determine the charge on each of the two capacitors after they are connected.
options: $6 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $18 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor
$8 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $16 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor
$16 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $8 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$ on the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor
questions.solution:
The initial charge on the first capacitor is:

$Q_1 = C_1 V_1 = 2.0 \times 12 = 24 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The second capacitor is initially uncharged, so $Q_2 = 0$. When the capacitors are connected, they are in parallel. The common potential $V$ across the combination is:

$V = \dfrac{\text{Total charge}}{\text{Total capacitance}} = \dfrac{Q_1 + Q_2}{C_1 + C_2}$

$V = \dfrac{24 + 0}{2.0 + 4.0} = \dfrac{24}{6.0} = 4 \text{ V}$

The final charge on the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:

$Q_1' = C_1 V = 2.0 \times 4 = 8 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

The final charge on the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:

$Q_2' = C_2 V = 4.0 \times 4 = 16 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor of capacitance $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ is charged to a potential difference of $12 \text{ V}$. It is then connected to an uncharged capacitor with a capacitance of $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the electrostatic energy stored in each of the two capacitors after the connection is established.
options: $16 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$ in the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $32 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$ in the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor
$48 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$ in the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $96 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$ in the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor
$32 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$ in the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor and $16 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$ in the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor
questions.solution:
Initial charge on the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:

$Q = C_1 V_1 = 2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times 12 \text{ V} = 24 \text{ }\mu\text{C}$

When it is connected to the uncharged $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor, the total charge is conserved and they reach a common potential $V$.

$V = \dfrac{\text{Total Charge}}{\text{Total Capacitance}} = \dfrac{Q}{C_1 + C_2}$

$V = \dfrac{24 \text{ }\mu\text{C}}{2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} + 4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}} = \dfrac{24}{6.0} \text{ V} = 4 \text{ V}$

The electrostatic energy stored in the $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:

$U_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} C_1 V^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times (4 \text{ V})^2 = 16 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$

The electrostatic energy stored in the $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is:

$U_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} C_2 V^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \times (4 \text{ V})^2 = 32 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor has its plates separated by $2.00 \text{ cm}$. An electron-proton pair is released at a certain position in the gap between the plates. It is observed that the proton arrives at the negative plate at the exact same instant the electron arrives at the positive plate. Determine the distance from the negative plate at which the pair was released.
questions.solution:
Let the pair be released at a distance $x$ from the negative plate. The proton moves towards the negative plate, covering a distance $x$, while the electron moves towards the positive plate, covering a distance $d - x$, where $d = 2.00 \text{ cm}$.

The acceleration of the proton is $a_p = \dfrac{eE}{m_p}$ and the acceleration of the electron is $a_e = \dfrac{eE}{m_e}$, where $E$ is the uniform electric field between the plates.

Since both particles start from rest and take the exact same time $t$ to reach their respective plates, we can write the equations of motion as:

$x = \dfrac{1}{2} a_p t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{eE}{m_p} \right) t^2$

$d - x = \dfrac{1}{2} a_e t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{eE}{m_e} \right) t^2$

Dividing the first equation by the second yields:

$\dfrac{x}{d - x} = \dfrac{m_e}{m_p}$

$x m_p = d m_e - x m_e$

$x = \dfrac{m_e d}{m_p + m_e}$

Since $m_p \gg m_e$, the denominator can be approximated as $m_p$, giving:

$x \approx \dfrac{m_e}{m_p} d$

Substituting the standard masses $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$, $m_p = 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$, and the distance $d = 2.00 \text{ cm}$:

$x \approx \dfrac{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \times 2.00 \text{ cm}$

$x \approx 10.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$

$x \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$

Answer: $1.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point charge $Q$ is located at the origin. Determine the electrostatic energy stored outside a sphere of radius $R$ that is centred at the origin.
questions.solution:
The electric field at a distance $r$ from a point charge $Q$ is given by $E = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$.

The electrostatic energy density in the region is $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$.

The total electrostatic energy stored outside a sphere of radius $R$ is obtained by integrating the energy density over the volume from $r = R$ to $r = \infty$.

$U = \int_{R}^{\infty} u \, dV$

Substituting $u$ and the volume element $dV = 4\pi r^2 dr$:

$U = \int_{R}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \left( \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \right)^2 4\pi r^2 dr$

$U = \int_{R}^{\infty} \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} dr$

$U = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{r} \right]_{R}^{\infty}$

$U = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor is charged to a potential difference of $12 \text{ V}$ and is subsequently connected to an uncharged $4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor. Determine the heat produced during the charge transfer from one capacitor to the other.
questions.solution:
Given:
$C_1 = 2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$
$V_1 = 12 \text{ V}$
$C_2 = 4.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$
$V_2 = 0 \text{ V}$

The heat produced during the charge transfer is equal to the loss in electrostatic potential energy of the system:

$\Delta H = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} (V_1 - V_2)^2$

Substituting the given values:

$\Delta H = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2.0 \times 4.0}{2.0 + 4.0} \times (12 - 0)^2 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$

$\Delta H = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{8.0}{6.0} \times 144 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$

$\Delta H = \dfrac{4}{6} \times 144 \text{ }\mu\text{J} = 96 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal sphere with a radius $R$ is charged to a potential $V$. Determine the electrostatic energy stored in the electric field inside a concentric sphere of radius $2R$.
questions.solution:
The potential of the metal sphere is $V = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R}$.

The charge on the sphere is $Q = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R V$.

The electric field at a distance $r$ from the center ($r > R$) is $E = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$. Since the electric field inside the metal sphere ($r < R$) is zero, the energy is stored only in the region $R < r < 2R$.

The energy density of the electric field is $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$.

The electrostatic energy stored in the region between $r = R$ and $r = 2R$ is given by integrating the energy density over the volume:

$U = \int_{R}^{2R} u \, dV = \int_{R}^{2R} \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \left( \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \right)^2 4\pi r^2 \, dr$

$U = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{R}^{2R} \dfrac{1}{r^2} \, dr = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{r} \right]_{R}^{2R}$

$U = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0} \left( \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{2R} \right) = \dfrac{Q^2}{16\pi\varepsilon_0 R}$

Substituting $Q = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R V$ into the expression for $U$:

$U = \dfrac{(4\pi\varepsilon_0 R V)^2}{16\pi\varepsilon_0 R} = \dfrac{16\pi^2\varepsilon_0^2 R^2 V^2}{16\pi\varepsilon_0 R} = \pi\varepsilon_0 R V^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal sphere with a radius $R$ is charged to a potential $V$. Let $U_1$ denote the electrostatic field energy stored inside a concentric sphere of radius $2R$, and $U_2$ denote the electrostatic field energy stored outside this sphere of radius $2R$. Determine the ratio $\dfrac{U_2}{U_1}$.
questions.solution:
The electric field at a distance $r$ from the center of the charged metal sphere ($r > R$) is given by $E = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r^2}$.

The energy density of the electric field is $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2 = \dfrac{Q^2}{32\pi^2\epsilon_0 r^4}$.

The energy stored in a spherical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ is $dU = u(4\pi r^2 dr) = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}dr$.

Since the electric field inside the metal sphere ($r < R$) is zero, the energy $U_1$ stored inside the concentric sphere of radius $2R$ corresponds to the region $R < r < 2R$:

$U_1 = \int_{R}^{2R} \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}dr = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0}\left[-\dfrac{1}{r}\right]_{R}^{2R} = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0}\left(\dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{2R}\right) = \dfrac{Q^2}{16\pi\epsilon_0 R}$

The energy $U_2$ stored outside the sphere of radius $2R$ corresponds to the region $2R < r < \infty$:

$U_2 = \int_{2R}^{\infty} \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}dr = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0}\left[-\dfrac{1}{r}\right]_{2R}^{\infty} = \dfrac{Q^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0}\left(0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{2R}\right)\right) = \dfrac{Q^2}{16\pi\epsilon_0 R}$

Therefore, the ratio $\dfrac{U_2}{U_1} = 1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large conducting plane possesses a surface charge density of $1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C m}^{-2}$. Determine the electrostatic energy stored within a cubical volume of edge $1.0 \text{ cm}$ situated in front of the plane.
questions.solution:
The electric field just outside a large conducting plane is given by:

$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}$

where $\sigma$ is the surface charge density.

The electrostatic energy density (energy stored per unit volume) in the electric field is:

$u = \dfrac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 E^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\epsilon_0}$

The volume of the cubical region is:

$V = a^3 = (1.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m})^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

The total electrostatic energy stored in this volume is:

$U = u \times V = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\epsilon_0} V$

Substituting the given values $\sigma = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C m}^{-2}$ and $\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F m}^{-1}$:

$U = \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$

$U = \dfrac{10^{-8} \times 10^{-6}}{17.7 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{10^{-14}}{17.7 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{10^{-2}}{17.7}$

$U \approx 5.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor with a plate area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ and a separation of $1.00 \text{ mm}$ between the plates is connected to a $12.0 \text{ V}$ battery. The plates are subsequently pulled apart to increase the separation to $2.0 \text{ mm}$. How much energy is absorbed by the battery during this process?
questions.solution:
The initial capacitance of the parallel-plate capacitor is given by:

$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_1}$

The final capacitance after the plates are pulled apart is:

$C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_2}$

Since the battery remains connected, the potential difference $V$ across the capacitor is constant. The initial and final charges on the capacitor are $Q_1 = C_1 V$ and $Q_2 = C_2 V$ respectively.

As the separation increases, the capacitance decreases, causing charge to flow back into the battery. The charge that flows back is:

$\Delta Q = Q_1 - Q_2 = (C_1 - C_2) V$

The energy absorbed by the battery is the work done on it by the returning charge:

$W = \Delta Q \times V = (C_1 - C_2) V^2$

Substituting the given values $A = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $d_1 = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $d_2 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, and $V = 12.0 \text{ V}$:

$C_1 = \dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 20 \times 10^{-4}}{1.00 \times 10^{-3}} = 17.7 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$

$C_2 = \dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 20 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$

The difference in capacitance is:

$C_1 - C_2 = 17.7 \times 10^{-12} - 8.85 \times 10^{-12} = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$

The energy absorbed by the battery is:

$W = (8.85 \times 10^{-12}) \times (12.0)^2$

$W = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 144 = 1274.4 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J}$

$W = 12.7 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor with a plate area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ and a separation of $1.00 \text{ mm}$ between the plates is connected to a $12.0 \text{ V}$ battery. The plates are subsequently pulled apart to increase the separation to $2.0 \text{ mm}$. Using the expression for the force between the plates, find the work done by the person pulling the plates apart.
questions.solution:
The force between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor connected to a constant voltage source $V$ is given by:

$F = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2}$

The work done by the external agent in pulling the plates apart from a separation $x_1$ to $x_2$ is:

$W = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F \, dx = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2} \, dx$

$W = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{x} \right]_{x_1}^{x_2} = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{x_1} - \dfrac{1}{x_2} \right)$

Given values are:

$A = 20 \text{ cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

$V = 12.0 \text{ V}$

$x_1 = 1.00 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$x_2 = 2.0 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

Substituting the values into the work equation:

$W = \dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 20 \times 10^{-4} \times (12.0)^2}{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} - \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \right)$

$W = 8.85 \times 10^{-15} \times 144 \times (1000 - 500)$

$W = 8.85 \times 10^{-15} \times 144 \times 500$

$W = 6.37 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J}$

Answer: $6.37 \times 10^{-10} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate capacitor with a plate area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ and a separation of $1.00 \text{ mm}$ between the plates is connected to a $12.0 \text{ V}$ battery. The plates are subsequently pulled apart to increase the separation to $2.0 \text{ mm}$. Which of the following statements correctly describes the heat produced during this transfer of charge as the separation is increased?
options: Heat is produced because the work done by the person is entirely dissipated as thermal energy.
Heat is produced because the energy absorbed by the battery is less than the decrease in the stored energy of the capacitor.
No heat is produced because the process occurs isothermally and the battery does zero work.
No heat is produced because the work done by the person plus the initial stored energy equals the final stored energy plus the energy absorbed by the battery.
questions.solution:
Let the initial capacitance be $C_i = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$. When the separation is increased to $2d$, the final capacitance becomes $C_f = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{2d} = \dfrac{C_i}{2}$.

The initial stored energy in the capacitor is $U_i = \dfrac{1}{2} C_i V^2$.

The final stored energy is $U_f = \dfrac{1}{2} C_f V^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} C_i V^2$.

The initial charge on the capacitor is $Q_i = C_i V$ and the final charge is $Q_f = C_f V = \dfrac{C_i V}{2}$.

Since the charge decreases, charge flows back to the battery. The energy absorbed by the battery is $\Delta Q \times V = \left(C_i V - \dfrac{C_i V}{2}\right) V = \dfrac{1}{2} C_i V^2$.

The attractive force between the plates at a separation $x$ is $F = \dfrac{Q^2}{2 \epsilon_0 A} = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2}$.

The work done by the external agent (person) to pull the plates apart is:

$W_{ext} = \int_{d}^{2d} F dx = \int_{d}^{2d} \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2} dx = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{x} \right]_{d}^{2d} = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{4 d} = \dfrac{1}{4} C_i V^2$

Applying the principle of conservation of energy:

$W_{ext} + U_i = U_f + \text{Energy absorbed by battery} + H$

$\dfrac{1}{4} C_i V^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} C_i V^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} C_i V^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} C_i V^2 + H$

Solving for $H$, we get $H = 0$.

Thus, no heat is produced because the work done by the person plus the initial stored energy equals the final stored energy plus the energy absorbed by the battery.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor with a capacitance of $100 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ is charged to a potential difference of $24 \text{ V}$. After disconnecting the charging battery, the capacitor is connected to a different battery of emf $12 \text{ V}$ such that the positive plate of the capacitor is attached to the positive terminal of the battery.
